findings.description: subprocess.run with shell=True interpolates attacker-controlled src/dest from the job payload, allowing arbitrary command injection.
attempt is reset to 0 at the top of every loop iteration before incrementing, so the while attempt < MAX_RETRIES guard never advances and a persistently failing job loops forever.
Because attempt is reset to 0 each iteration, the backoff RETRY_BACKOFF_SECONDS * (attempt + 1) is always constant and never grows as the intended progressive/exponential backoff.
Blocking time.sleep() inside the async poll_loop stalls the entire event loop instead of yielding; should be await asyncio.sleep().
run_with_retries uses blocking time.sleep and is invoked from the async poll_loop, blocking the event loop during every retry backoff.
poll_loop runs while True with no cancellation or stop flag, so with stop_after=None it never terminates except via external task cancellation.
pop(0) on a list is O(n); a FIFO queue should use collections.deque for O(1) dequeue.
subprocess result stderr/stdout is captured but discarded, so failures of the convert command are reported only as a boolean with no diagnostic logging.
sum(values) raises TypeError if the payload values list contains non-numeric items, surfacing only as a generic job failure.
Bare except Exception swallows all errors (including programming bugs) into a generic retry, hiding the failure category.
When the queue stays empty, poll_loop sleeps indefinitely and never reaches the stop_after termination check, so stop_after is not honored without inbound jobs.
JobQueue.pop/push are not thread- or task-safe, which is risky if the sync drain and async poll loop ever share a queue concurrently.
drain_sync return type is annotated as bare dict rather than a precise dict[str, int], unlike the typed annotations used elsewhere.
__init__ methods lack return type annotations (-> None) that the rest of the module otherwise applies consistently.
